content:
# Exodus Chapter 39 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 110-112; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 82-83 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_039_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_039_page_002.jpg`, `EXO_039_page_003.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_039_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_039_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Woven Service-Garments (v. 1)

**1** And from the blue and the purple and the scarlet yarn they made garments of service for ministering in the holy place, and they made the holy garments that were for Aaron, as YHWH commanded Moses.

---

## The Ephod (vv. 2-7)

**2** And he made the ephod of gold and blue and purple and scarlet yarn and twisted linen.

**3** And they hammered out the plates of the gold, and he cut threads to work in the midst of the blue and in the midst of the purple and in the midst of the scarlet yarn and in the midst of the linen -- the work of a designer.

**4** Shoulder-pieces they made for it, joined together; upon its two ends it was joined.

**5** And the band of his ephod that was on it -- from it it was, according to its workmanship -- gold and blue and purple and scarlet yarn and twisted linen, as YHWH commanded Moses.

**6** And they made the onyx stones, enclosed in settings of gold, engraved with the engravings of a signet, upon the names of the Sons of Israel.

**7** And he set them upon the shoulder-pieces of the ephod -- stones of memorial for the Sons of Israel -- as YHWH commanded Moses.

---

## The Breastpiece (vv. 8-21)

**8** And he made the breastpiece -- the work of a designer, like the work of the ephod -- gold and blue and purple and scarlet yarn and twisted linen.

**9** It was square; doubled they made the breastpiece -- a span its length and a span its width, doubled.

**10** And they set in it four rows of stone: a row -- a ruby, a topaz, and an emerald -- the first row.

**11** And the second row -- a turquoise, a sapphire, and a diamond.

**12** And the third row -- a jacinth, an agate, and an amethyst.

**13** And the fourth row -- a beryl, an onyx, and a jasper; enclosed in settings of gold in their mountings.

**14** And the stones were upon the names of the Sons of Israel -- twelve, upon their names -- engravings of a signet, each man upon his name, for the twelve tribes.

**15** And they made upon the breastpiece chains of twisted work, the work of braided cord, of pure gold.

**16** And they made two settings of gold and two rings of gold, and they put the two rings upon the two ends of the breastpiece.

**17** And they put the two braided chains of gold upon the two rings upon the ends of the breastpiece.

**18** And the two ends of the two braided chains they put upon the two settings, and they put them upon the shoulder-pieces of the ephod, toward its face.

**19** And they made two rings of gold, and they set them upon the two ends of the breastpiece, upon its edge that was toward the ephod, inward.

**20** And they made two rings of gold, and they put them upon the two shoulder-pieces of the ephod, from beneath, from its face, close to its joining, above the band of the ephod.

**21** And they bound the breastpiece from its rings unto the rings of the ephod with a cord of blue, to be upon the band of the ephod, and so that the breastpiece would not be displaced from the ephod -- as YHWH commanded Moses.

---

## The Robe of the Ephod (vv. 22-26)

**22** And he made the robe of the ephod -- the work of a weaver -- entirely of blue.

**23** And the opening of the robe in its midst, like the opening of a coat of mail -- a border for its opening all around; it would not be torn.

**24** And they made upon the hems of the robe pomegranates of blue and purple and scarlet yarn, twisted.

**25** And they made bells of pure gold, and they put the bells in the midst of the pomegranates, upon the hems of the robe all around, in the midst of the pomegranates:

**26** a bell and a pomegranate, a bell and a pomegranate, upon the hems of the robe all around, for ministering -- as YHWH commanded Moses.

---

## The Tunics, Turban, and Undergarments (vv. 27-29)

**27** And they made the tunics of linen -- the work of a weaver -- for Aaron and for his sons.

**28** And the turban of linen, and the ornamental headbands of linen, and the undergarments of linen -- twisted linen,

**29** and the sash of twisted linen and blue and purple and scarlet yarn -- the work of an embroiderer -- as YHWH commanded Moses.

---

## The Holy Crown (vv. 30-31)

**30** And they made the plate of the holy crown of pure gold, and they wrote upon it a writing of the engravings of a signet: "Holy to YHWH."

**31** And they put upon it a cord of blue to set it upon the turban from above -- as YHWH commanded Moses.

---

## The Work Completed and Brought to Moses (vv. 32-43)

**32** And all the work of the tabernacle of the tent of meeting was completed; and the Sons of Israel did according to all that YHWH commanded Moses -- so they did.

**33** And they brought the tabernacle unto Moses: the tent and all its vessels -- its clasps, its boards, its bars, and its pillars, and its bases;

**34** and the covering of the rams' skins dyed red, and the covering of the takhash skins, and the veil of the screen;

**35** the ark of the testimony and its poles and the mercy-seat;

**36** the table, all its vessels and the bread of the Presence;

**37** the pure lampstand, its lamps -- lamps of the arrangement -- and all its vessels and the oil of the light;

**38** and the golden altar, and the anointing oil, and the incense of the spices, and the screen of the entrance of the tent;

**39** the bronze altar and the bronze grating that was for it, its poles and all its vessels; the basin and its stand;

**40** the hangings of the courtyard, its pillars and its bases, and the screen for the gate of the courtyard, its cords and its pegs, and all the vessels of the service of the tabernacle for the tent of meeting;

**41** the garments of service for ministering in the holy place -- the holy garments for Aaron the priest, and the garments of his sons for serving as priests.

**42** According to all that YHWH commanded Moses, so the Sons of Israel did all the work.

**43** And Moses saw all the work, and behold, they had done it -- as YHWH had commanded, so they had done -- and Moses blessed them.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Manuscript Observations

The Leningrad Codex (B19A) presents Exodus 39 across pages 110-112 in the standard three-column format with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation. The chapter opens on folio 110, continuing from the material accounting of chapter 38. The chapter divides into two major sections: the fabrication of the priestly garments (vv. 1-31), fulfilling the instructions of Exodus 28, and the presentation of the completed tabernacle work to Moses (vv. 32-43), which concludes the entire construction narrative begun in chapter 35. A parashah setumah break is visible before verse 32, marking the transition from individual garment descriptions to the comprehensive completion formula. The vocalization of the gemstone names in verses 10-13 is fully legible, including several rare forms (leshem, shevo, akhlamah, tarshish, yashfeh). The refrain ka'asher tsivvah YHWH et-Mosheh ("as YHWH commanded Moses") appears seven times in the garment section (vv. 1, 5, 7, 21, 26, 29, 31) and recurs in the completion section (vv. 32, 42, 43), forming the chapter's structural skeleton. The plene and defective spellings are consistent with standard Leningrad practice throughout. Folio 112 shows the chapter's conclusion with the detailed inventory list of vv. 33-41 and the climactic blessing of v. 43.

The Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209, pp. 82-83) presents significant structural divergence from the MT in this chapter. On page 82, the three-column uncial scriptio continua contains Greek text corresponding to portions of the priestly garment construction, with gemstone lists and attachment mechanism descriptions visible in recognizable form. Page 83, headed CAP. XXXIX, presents material corresponding more closely to the completion and inspection pericope (MT 39:32-43) along with anointing and consecration material. The critical structural divergence is that much of the MT's priestly garment fabrication (39:1-31) was placed earlier in LXX chapter 36, integrated with the original garment instructions. The LXX's chapter 39 thus contains primarily the completion, assembly, and inspection material -- corresponding roughly to MT 39:32-43 and parts of MT 40. The content is ultimately the same across both traditions, but the arrangement reflects different editorial approaches to the relationship between divine instruction and human execution. The gemstone identifications in the LXX diverge from the MT in several cases (sardion for odem, smaragdos for bareqet, anthrax for nofekh), reflecting the difficulty ancient translators faced with mineralogical terminology. The completion formula in v. 43 is preserved faithfully: kai eiden Mouses panta ta erga ("and Moses saw all the works") and eulogesen autous Mouses ("and Moses blessed them"), maintaining the creation-echo that is central to the verse's theology.

### Theological Architecture of the Chapter

Exodus 39 is the culmination of the entire construction narrative (Exodus 35-39) and the fulfillment of the garment instructions given in Exodus 28. The chapter's two-part structure -- garment fabrication (vv. 1-31) and completion-inspection (vv. 32-43) -- brings the tabernacle project to its climax.

**The Sevenfold Obedience Refrain:** The phrase "as YHWH commanded Moses" appears seven times within the garment section (vv. 1, 5, 7, 21, 26, 29, 31), punctuating each major garment element: the opening frame (v. 1), the ephod's waistband (v. 5), the shoulder stones (v. 7), the breastpiece attachment (v. 21), the robe's bells and pomegranates (v. 26), the sash (v. 29), and the golden plate (v. 31). This sevenfold repetition mirrors the seven divine speeches of creation ("And God said," Genesis 1:3, 6, 9, 14, 20, 24, 26) and stamps the priestly garments with the mark of perfect obedience. Each garment element is sealed with the refrain, building toward the comprehensive completion statement of verse 32.

**The Creation-Tabernacle Parallel:** The chapter's conclusion deliberately recapitulates the creation account of Genesis 1:31-2:3. The parallels are precise: (a) "all the work was completed" (va-tekhel kol-avodat, 39:32) echoes "the heavens and the earth were completed" (va-yekhullu ha-shamayim ve-ha-arets, Gen 2:1); (b) "Moses saw all the work" (va-yar Mosheh et-kol ha-melakhah, 39:43) echoes "God saw all that he had made" (va-yar Elohim et-kol asher asah, Gen 1:31); (c) "and behold, they had done it" (ve-hinneh asu otah, 39:43) echoes "and behold, it was very good" (ve-hinneh tov me'od, Gen 1:31); (d) "and Moses blessed them" (va-yevarekh otam Mosheh, 39:43) echoes "and God blessed the seventh day" (va-yevarekh Elohim et-yom ha-shevi'i, Gen 2:3). The tabernacle is thus a microcosm -- a re-creation of sacred space on earth -- and its completion recapitulates the completion of the cosmos itself.

**The Priest as Living Tabernacle:** The priestly garments of Exodus 39 transform the human body into a mobile sanctuary. The ephod and breastpiece employ the same materials as the tabernacle curtains (26:1): gold, blue, purple, scarlet, and twisted linen. The golden bells echo the golden furnishings. The blue robe mirrors the blue of the heavenly realm. The golden plate inscribed "Holy to YHWH" corresponds to the ark's mercy-seat -- the most sacred element placed at the highest point. When fully vested, the high priest embodies the sacred space: he carries Israel's names on his shoulders (strength) and over his heart (love), bears the holiness inscription on his brow, and moves through the sanctuary as its human counterpart. The garments do not merely adorn; they consecrate.

**The Garment-Instruction Symmetry:** Exodus 39 mirrors Exodus 28 with near-verbatim fidelity: ephod (28:6-14 / 39:2-7), breastpiece (28:15-30 / 39:8-21), robe (28:31-35 / 39:22-26), tunics and sash (28:39 / 39:27-29), golden plate (28:36-38 / 39:30-31). The repetition is not editorial carelessness but a deliberate literary strategy: it demonstrates that Israel built exactly what God specified, neither adding nor subtracting.

**Moses' Blessing:** The unspecified blessing of verse 43 bridges chapters 39 and 40 -- from human completion to divine habitation. Rabbinic tradition identifies it with the prayer that the Shekhinah may rest upon the work of their hands. The narrative logic supports this: the tabernacle has been built precisely according to command, inspected and approved; what remains is for God to fill it with glory. The workers have done their part; they await the descent of the divine presence.

### Key Consensus Decisions

1. **"Garments of service" for bigdei serad (vv. 1, 41):** EMET renders "garments of service/woven-service," LOGOS renders "garments of service," PNEUMA renders "finely woven garments for ministry." SOPHIA adopts "garments of service" (EMET/LOGOS) because the Hebrew serad, while its root is uncertain, designates garments defined by their function (service in the sanctuary) rather than by their fabrication method. PNEUMA's "finely woven" adds an interpretive specification of the textile process not certain from the Hebrew root.

2. **"Designer" for khoshev (vv. 3, 8):** EMET renders "designer," LOGOS renders "skilled craftsman," PNEUMA renders "master craftsman." SOPHIA adopts "designer" (EMET) because khoshev is a participle of khashav ("to think, plan, devise") and designates one who conceives and executes complex designs. "Skilled craftsman" (LOGOS) and "master craftsman" (PNEUMA) add intensifiers ("skilled," "master") not present in the Hebrew noun and flatten the specific connotation of design-thinking that the root khashav carries. This is consistent with SOPHIA's rendering in Exodus 26:1, 28:6, and 28:15.

3. **"Displaced" for yizzakh (v. 21):** EMET renders "displaced," LOGOS renders "slip," PNEUMA renders "come loose." SOPHIA adopts "displaced" (EMET) because the Hebrew root zakhakh means "to move away from proper position" -- a more precise term than the casual "slip" (LOGOS) or the generic "come loose" (PNEUMA). The breastpiece must not be displaced from the ephod; it must remain in its ordained position. "Displaced" conveys both the spatial and the theological impropriety of separation.

4. **"Entirely" for kelil (v. 22):** EMET renders "all of," LOGOS renders "entirely," PNEUMA renders "entirely." SOPHIA adopts "entirely" (LOGOS/PNEUMA majority) because the Hebrew kelil means "whole, complete, entirely" -- the robe is wholly of blue, without the mixture of materials that characterizes other garments. "Entirely of blue" reads more naturally than "all of blue."

5. **"Border" for safah (v. 23):** EMET renders "lip," LOGOS renders "border," PNEUMA renders "reinforced binding." SOPHIA adopts "border" (LOGOS) because while the Hebrew safah literally means "lip," in this architectural/textile context it designates the reinforced edge around the robe's opening. "Lip" (EMET) preserves the bodily metaphor but may confuse English readers; "reinforced binding" (PNEUMA) adds "reinforced" beyond what the Hebrew states. "Border" conveys the woven edge without addition or obscurity.

6. **"Coat of mail" for takhara (v. 23):** All three translators agree on "coat of mail" or "coat of armor." SOPHIA adopts "coat of mail" (EMET/LOGOS) as the standard rendering of takhara, a hapax legomenon that designates a leather or metal protective garment with a reinforced collar opening.

7. **"Ornamental headbands" for pa'arei ha-migba'ot (v. 28):** EMET renders "headbands of the caps," LOGOS renders "ornamental caps," PNEUMA renders "ornamental headbands." SOPHIA adopts "ornamental headbands" -- combining EMET's "headbands" with the "ornamental" qualifier that both LOGOS and PNEUMA include. The Hebrew pa'ar means "to beautify, to ornament," and the migba'ot are the headpieces of the ordinary priests. "Ornamental headbands" conveys both the decorative function (pa'ar) and the form (migba'ot as head-coverings distinct from the high priest's turban).

8. **"Takhash skins" for orot ha-tekhashim (v. 34):** EMET renders "takhash skins," LOGOS renders "tachash skins," PNEUMA renders "fine leather." SOPHIA adopts "takhash skins" (EMET) because tekhashim designates an unidentified animal or material -- the identification remains debated (dugong, dolphin, fine leather). Transliterating preserves the uncertainty rather than resolving it into a specific identification. "Fine leather" (PNEUMA) is a guess that forecloses scholarly debate. This is consistent with SOPHIA's rendering in previous chapters.

9. **"Veil of the screen" for parokhet ha-masakh (v. 34):** EMET renders "curtain of the screen," LOGOS renders "veil of the screen," PNEUMA renders "shielding curtain." SOPHIA adopts "veil of the screen" (LOGOS), consistent with the decision in Exodus 38:27 to render parokhet as "veil" -- preserving its unique function as the concealing barrier between the Holy Place and the Most Holy Place. PNEUMA's "shielding curtain" collapses two distinct Hebrew terms (parokhet and masakh) into a single phrase.

10. **"Basin" for kiyyor (v. 39):** Consistent with the Exodus 38:8 decision, SOPHIA renders kiyyor as "basin" (LOGOS/PNEUMA majority) rather than "laver" (EMET), prioritizing modern comprehension.

11. **"Serving as priests" for le-khahen (v. 41):** EMET renders "acting-as-priests," LOGOS renders "serving as priests," PNEUMA renders "to serve as priests." SOPHIA adopts "serving as priests" (LOGOS) because the Piel infinitive le-khahen designates the ongoing performance of priestly duties, and "serving as priests" captures both the verbal force (active service) and the priestly identity without EMET's hyphenated compound or PNEUMA's infinitive construction.

12. **"And behold" for ve-hinneh (v. 43):** EMET renders "and behold," LOGOS renders "and behold," PNEUMA renders "and indeed." SOPHIA adopts "and behold" (EMET/LOGOS) because hinneh is the Hebrew presentative particle of discovery and disclosure -- "behold" captures its function of drawing the reader's attention to what Moses discovers upon inspection. "Indeed" (PNEUMA) is a confirmatory adverb that loses the visual, disclosive force of hinneh. The creation-echo is preserved: "and behold, they had done it" parallels "and behold, it was very good" (Gen 1:31).

13. **"Twisted linen" for shesh moshzar (vv. 2, 5, 8, 24, 28, 29):** Consistent with SOPHIA's established rendering throughout the construction narrative, shesh moshzar is rendered "twisted linen" -- translating what the Hebrew states without the addition of "fine" (LOGOS) or the substitution of "woven" for "twisted" (PNEUMA).

14. **"Work of a weaver" for ma'aseh oreg (vv. 22, 27):** All three translators agree. SOPHIA concurs. The Hebrew oreg designates the weaver's craft (plain weaving), distinguished from the designer's work (khoshev, complex patterned weaving) and the embroiderer's work (roqem, needle-work).

15. **"Work of an embroiderer" for ma'aseh roqem (v. 29):** Consistent with SOPHIA's rejection of PNEUMA's added intensifiers throughout the construction narrative, "work of an embroiderer" preserves the Hebrew without supplementary qualifiers.
